brief_justification: This article provides a qualitative policy review ('balanço') of social policies under the Lula and Dilma governments (2003-2016), covering Bolsa Família, social security, minimum wage, health (SUS), and higher education access. It contains no tables, figures, or equations, and employs no quantitative methods, statistical analysis, or causal research design. The analysis is essayistic, drawing on secondary sources and policy documents to argue that these governments improved conditions for the poorest via income transfers while leaving structural causes of poverty unchanged and consolidating private sector roles in social policy.
